perturbation.original_text: All obligations of Receiving Party with respect to any Conf idential Information received from Disclosing Party under the terms of this Agreement shall s urvive and continue for five (5) years after the date of the last discl osure of Confidential Informati on by Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: All obligations of Receiving Party with respect to any Confidential Information received from Disclosing Party under the terms of this Agreement shall generally continue for five (5) years after what they consider to be the date of the last disclosure of Confidential Information by Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'generally' introduces an exception to the five-year survival period. The addition of 'what they consider to be' adds subjectivity and ambiguity as to when the obligations end. The clause 'generally' creates a loophole for the Receiving Party to potentially claim that the obligations have expired sooner than five years, undermining the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules § 213
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 213 sets a six-year statute of limitations for actions upon a contractual obligation or liability, express or implied. By creating ambiguity around the five-year survival period, the modification indirectly conflicts with the principle of a clear statute of limitations, as the actual duration of the NDA's obligations becomes uncertain and potentially shorter than the period during which a breach could be legally pursued, thus undermining the statute of limitations.

Civil Practice Law & Rules (CVP) CHAPTER 8, ARTICLE 2 § 213. Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property;

Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property; based on mistake; by corporation against director, officer or stockholder; based on fraud. The following actions must be commenced within six years: 1. an action for which no limitation is specifically prescribed by law; 2. an action upon a contractual obligation or liability, express or implied, except as provided in section two hundred thirteen-a or two hundred fourteen-i of this article or article 2 of the uniform commercial code or article 36-B of the general business law; 3. an action upon a sealed instrument; 4. an action upon a bond or note, the payment of which is secured by a mortgage upon real property, or upon a bond or note and mortgage so secured, or upon a mortgage of real property, or any interest therein; (a) In any action on an instrument described under this subdivision, if the statute of limitations is raised as a defense, and if that defense is based on a claim that the instrument at issue was accelerated
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the duration of the Receiving Party's obligations, which contradicts the principle of a clear statute of limitations for contractual obligations. The original text specifies a five-year survival period, while the changed text uses 'generally' and 'what they consider to be,' making the duration uncertain. NY CPLR § 213, as indicated in both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Snippet 2, mandates a six-year statute of limitations for actions upon a contractual obligation. By creating ambiguity and a potential loophole, the modified text could lead to disputes over when the NDA's obligations expire, potentially undermining the six-year statute of limitations because the actual duration of the NDA's obligations becomes uncertain and potentially shorter than the period during which a breach could be legally pursued.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any action for breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party or its Representatives, th e prevailing party shall be entitled to reasonable attorneys’ fees, costs and expenses incurred in such action.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of any action for breach of this Agreement by Receiving Party or its Representatives, the prevailing party may be entitled to attorneys’ fees subject to further negotiation and approval by a mutually agreed-upon mediator.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be entitled' to 'may be entitled' makes the awarding of attorneys' fees discretionary rather than mandatory. Adding 'subject to further negotiation and approval by a mutually agreed-upon mediator' adds significant ambiguity and hurdles to recovering attorneys' fees, making it less certain that the prevailing party will actually receive them, even in clear cases of breach.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules § 8101
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 8101 generally discusses costs awarded in actions. While it does not mandate attorney fees, by making attorney fees discretionary and subject to mediation after a prevailing party has been determined, the amended clause weakens the ability of the prevailing party to recover full costs and expenses, potentially discouraging legitimate claims and complicating the enforcement of contractual rights. The added steps can be seen as undermining the principle of fair compensation to the prevailing party as outlined in CPLR.

Costs in an action. The party in whose favor a judgment is entered is entitled to costs in the action, unless otherwise provided by statute or unless the court determines that to so allow costs would not be equitable, under all of the circumstances.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates attorneys' fees for the prevailing party ('shall be entitled'), while the changed text makes it discretionary ('may be entitled') and adds conditions ('subject to further negotiation and approval by a mutually agreed-upon mediator'). This change directly undermines the principle outlined in NY CPLR § 8101, as presented in both scraped snippets, which states that 'the party in whose favor a judgment is entered is entitled to costs in the action, unless otherwise provided by statute or unless the court determines that to so allow costs would not be equitable, under all of the circumstances.' The modified text introduces ambiguity and additional hurdles that contradict this entitlement, creating a significant ambiguity that a prevailing party may not recover costs.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party may disclose the Confidential Information of Disclosing Party pursuant to a valid order issued by a court or governmental agency; prov ided that Receiving Party only discloses that portion of the Confidential Information that is specifical ly required by the order, and further provided that Receiving Party provides Disclosing Party with: (a) prior written notice of such order, and (b) reasonable opportunity to oppose such disclosure or obtain a pr otective order or similar relief.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party may disclose the Confidential Information of Disclosing Party pursuant to what they perceive as a valid order issued by a court or governmental agency; provided that Receiving Party discloses what they believe to be the portion of the Confidential Information specifically required by the order, and provided that Receiving Party attempts to provide Disclosing Party with: (a) notice of such order, and (b) a reasonable opportunity, if feasible, to oppose such disclosure or obtain a protective order or similar relief.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications inject subjectivity into the decision-making process regarding disclosure. The phrases 'what they perceive as a valid order' and 'what they believe to be the portion' replace objective criteria with the Receiving Party's subjective assessment. Removing 'prior written' before 'notice' allows for verbal notice. Changing 'reasonable opportunity' to 'reasonable opportunity, if feasible' makes it optional, allowing the Receiving Party to bypass the Disclosing Party's rights if they deem it inconvenient. These changes create significant ambiguity and weaken the protections for Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules § 2304
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 2304 addresses motions to compel compliance with a subpoena. By allowing the Receiving Party to subjectively determine the validity and scope of the order, and by weakening the notification requirement, the modified clause undermines the Disclosing Party's ability to challenge or limit the scope of a subpoena for confidential information, as contemplated under CPLR § 2304. The phrase 'if feasible' gives too much discretion to the Receiving Party in deciding whether to allow the Disclosing Party to quash or modify the subpoena.

Motion to quash, fix conditions or modify. A motion to quash, fix conditions or modify a subpoena shall be made promptly in the court in which the subpoena is returnable. If the subpoena is not returnable in a court, a request to withdraw or modify the subpoena shall first be made to the person who issued it and a motion to quash, fix conditions or modify may thereafter be made in the supreme court; except that such motion with respect to a child support subpoena issued pursuant to section one hundred eleven-p of the social services law shall be made to a judge of the family court or the supreme court. Reasonable conditions may be imposed upon the granting or denial of a motion to quash or modify.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and subjectivity, weakening the protections afforded to the Disclosing Party's confidential information, potentially undermining their ability to challenge a subpoena. The original contract language used objective standards ("". .valid order", "specifically required"), whereas the modified version replaces these with the Receiving Party's subjective assessment ("". .perceive as a valid order", "what they believe to be the portion"). Furthermore, the removal of 'prior written' from the notice requirement and the addition of 'if feasible' significantly erode the Disclosing Party's opportunity to oppose disclosure. This undermines the Disclosing Party's ability to challenge or modify a subpoena, a right contemplated by NY CPLR § 2304, as mentioned in both scraped snippets, which states: 'A motion to quash, fix conditions or modify a subpoena shall be made promptly in the court in which the subpoena is returnable'. The modified language allows the Receiving Party to potentially bypass providing adequate or timely notice, thus creating a clear contradiction.
